items.myth: A tree and a graph are different things.
items.reality: A tree IS a graph — specifically a connected, undirected, acyclic one with a designated root. Graph algorithms (DFS, BFS) work on trees as a special case.
items.example:
```python
# Same code shape works on both:
# DFS over a tree (no cycles) and DFS over a general graph (with visited set).
```

items.myth: BFS always finds the shortest path.
items.reality: BFS finds the path with the *fewest edges*, which is shortest only when all edges have equal weight. Use Dijkstra for weighted graphs.
items.example:
```python
# A -1-> B -1-> C  is cheaper than  A -100-> C
# BFS picks A -> C (1 edge); Dijkstra picks A -> B -> C (cost 2).
```

items.myth: Adjacency matrices are always slower than lists.
items.reality: For dense graphs (E close to V²), a matrix uses similar memory and gives O(1) edge-existence checks. Lists win for sparse graphs (the common case in knowledge graphs and ML systems).
items.example:
```python
# 1000 nodes, ~1000 edges -> list wins.
# 1000 nodes, ~500_000 edges -> matrix is competitive.
```

items.myth: Dijkstra also works with negative edges if you just allow them.
items.reality: Negative edges break Dijkstra's greedy invariant — a node popped from the priority queue can still get a shorter path later. Use Bellman-Ford for negative weights.
items.example:
```python
# Edge A->B = 5, A->C = 1, C->B = -10.
# Dijkstra pops B at cost 5 and locks it in, but the true shortest is 1 + (-10) = -9.
```

items.myth: All cycles are bad and should be avoided.
items.reality: Cycles are required for many designs — agent loops that retry until a condition is met, retrieval pipelines that iteratively refine. Just don't run cycle-intolerant algorithms (topo-sort, naive DFS without visited) on cyclic graphs.
items.example:
```python
# LangGraph deliberately allows cycles so agents can loop until 'done'.
```
